query: treatment options for laryngeal carcinoma involving arytenoids and aryepiglottic folds

retrieved_docs.title: Subglottic Stenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Patients who require frequent dilation intervals and wish to avoid the potential complications associated with open-airway surgery may opt for endoscopic laryngotracheoplasty, also known as the Maddern procedure. The Maddern procedure involves the complete resection of the subglottic mucosa, including the scar, while preserving the cartilaginous framework. [29] Although comprehensive long-term outcomes have not been extensively studied, this approach may provide another viable option for patients before considering open-airway surgery.

retrieved_docs.title: Glottic Stenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The management of glottic stenosis is variable and complicated, and it requires individualized treatment by experts in laryngeal pathologies. The primary concern is to secure the patient’s airway. However, voice and laryngeal function preservation are of nearly equal significance.

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Glottic Insufficiency -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: The differential diagnosis for glottic insufficiency or airway glottic insufficiency includes the following conditions: Laryngeal cancer Vocal cord nodules and polyps Acute corditis vocalis Hypotonic voice disorders Reinke's edema Laryngeal granuloma Functional aphonia Spasmodic dysphonia Dysphonia plicae ventricularis Hypotonic voice disorders Essential tremor

retrieved_docs.title: Total Laryngectomy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Many surgeons plan on an immediate direct laryngoscopy after the airway has been secured to confirm the location and extent of the tumor and to plan the location of the initial pharyngotomy during the laryngectomy (into the pyriform versus the vallecula).

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngomalacia -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngeal papillomatosis may cause a hoarse cry with upper airway obstruction, which may present early in infancy, and diagnosis is by either flexible fiberoptic laryngoscopy or direct laryngoscopy and biopsy of the lesions.

retrieved_docs.title: Supraglottic Airway Obstruction -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Angioedema Foreign body Ludwig's angina Supraglottic, epiglottic or neck abscess Tumor

retrieved_docs.title: Upper Airway Obstruction -- Evaluation -- Flexible Laryngoscopy
retrieved_docs.content: No comprehensive ENT examination is complete without flexible nasendoscopy/ laryngoscopy to dynamically assess the pharynx and larynx. This can help diagnose turbinate inflammation, septal deviation, adenoidal hypertrophy, Waldeyer’s ring hypertrophy, or laryngeal/hypopharyngeal pathology. It is particularly useful when assessing the dynamic function of the larynx. A dynamic assessment is essential when assessing vocal cord movement and relatively common conditions affecting the larynx, such as laryngomalacia in children. It can also diagnose a laryngeal tumor in adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngotracheal Stenosis -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Some differential diagnoses that must be considered in the evaluation of laryngotracheal stenosis include the following: [22] Angioedema Asthma Epiglottitis Esophageal tumor Foreign body aspiration Gastroesophageal reflux disease Mediastinal mass Retrosternal goiter Subglottic web Tracheomalacia Vocal cord dysfunction

retrieved_docs.title: Direct Laryngoscopy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Successful direct laryngoscopy involves multiple steps. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal Cancer -- Surgical Oncology -- Endoscopic and Transoral Surgery
retrieved_docs.content: Supracricoid laryngectomy: This procedure is used for T3 and select T4 tumors of the glottis or supraglottis that are not amenable to supraglottic laryngectomy due to involvement of 1 or both vocal folds but spare at least 1 arytenoid. Resection includes the thyroid cartilage, both false vocal folds, and can include the ipsilateral true cord and arytenoid but must spare at least a functioning arytenoid. [63] Once the resection is performed, the remaining larynx is then suspended to the hyoid (cricohyoidopexy) or epiglottis (unless this was resected-cricohyoepiglottopexy) to maximize postoperative swallowing function. [63] [64] A tracheostomy is required at the time of surgery and may also be required permanently. The voice outcomes are commonly described as poor after supracricoid laryngectomy, although they may be quite similar to their immediate preoperative voice. Most patients can phonate intelligibly and use a telephone. [65]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Larynx Cartilage -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As discussed, it is essential to understand the anatomy of the laryngeal cartilages as pathology can cause airway compromise. Pediatricians must be aware of this when dealing with epiglottitis and croup. Surgeons, anesthetists, or emergency physicians attempting to gain emergency airway access in cases of ‘cannot intubate, cannot ventilate’ require a working knowledge of the surface anatomy of the thyroid and cricoid cartilages. This knowledge allows identification of the cricothyroid membrane and a cricothyroidotomy to insert an infraglottic endotracheal tube. Otolaryngologists must have a detailed understanding of the laryngeal cartilages as anatomical landmarks during surgery and as sites of invasion of laryngeal cancer that significantly impact staging, prognosis, and management.

retrieved_docs.content: The laryngoscope is then moved anteriorly to reveal the vocal cords.

retrieved_docs.title: Glottic Stenosis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Failure of endoscopic procedures or thicker laryngeal webs requires open surgical techniques. A laryngofissure is necessary with a subsequent open laryngeal repair with dissection of the web. A laryngeal stent or Silastic keel is often used to keep the laryngeal lumen open while the mucosal surfaces are healing. Various grafts have been used to cover the raw area, such as skin grafts, endonasal or buccal mucosa, and perichondrium-cutaneous composite grafts. Rib cartilage grafts can be used to widen the glottis and subglottis. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Airway Glottic Insufficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Restoring glottic competence can enhance voice quality, alleviate dysphagia, and lower the risk of aspiration. Addressing these symptoms can improve a patient's overall quality of life. To tailor an appropriate treatment approach, management decisions should consider patient characteristics such as age, occupation, social circumstances, and individual preferences.

retrieved_docs.title: Laryngeal and Tracheal Stents -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Laryngeal and tracheal stents are crucial in managing complex airway conditions, offering mechanical support, and preventing collapse in patients with stenosis, tumors, or scar tissue. While congenital and acquired factors contribute to laryngotracheal pathologies, various treatment approaches, including endoscopic stent placement, aim to alleviate airway obstruction. Many types of stents exist, each with its indications and complications. Proper stent selection is paramount to optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2146
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, indicating that certain respiratory interventions that are not directly therapeutic to the larynx can nonetheless involve the larynx indirectly. This relationship highlights the importance of recognizing the larynx’s involvement when planning and performing non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, as indirect effects on the larynx may impact airway function and patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx is classified as a throat surgery and encompasses the specific procedure تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, which is listed as a child of Injection of larynx. In addition, Injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea is also categorized as a throat surgery. Both interventions involve therapeutic injections within the upper airway and represent distinct subtypes of throat surgery, illustrating the variety of surgical options available for managing laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx, indicating that these examinations focus on assessing the structure and function of the larynx (often in conjunction with the adjacent trachea) as part of otolaryngologic evaluation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1224
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly related to the larynx and are organized as a subcategory of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum. According to the indirect_procedure_site_of relationship, the larynx also serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. This dual classification highlights the larynx’s central role both as the primary anatomical focus of specific diagnostic interventions and as a secondary site affected by broader non‑therapeutic respiratory procedures, emphasizing its importance in the overall assessment and management of respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 143
retrieved_docs.content: is likewise associated with the thoracic cavity, emphasizing that bronchial cancers arise within this compartment. Collectively, these entities illustrate a hierarchical framework in which anatomical structures (larynx, trachea, bronchus, thoracic cavity) and procedural categories (diagnostic, therapeutic, non‑therapeutic, throat surgery) are interrelated to guide respiratory system care and thoracic oncology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2332
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are directly associated with the larynx, indicating that the larynx is the primary anatomical focus of these examinations. In addition, the larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑OR therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system, meaning that it can be involved secondarily in various non‑operative respiratory treatments. Understanding that the larynx functions both as the main target for diagnostic evaluation of the larynx and trachea and as an indirect site for additional respiratory therapies is essential for comprehensive patient assessment and coordinated care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1313
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect site for various other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. Within this broader category, the specific intervention described as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway) is classified as a child procedure. Recognizing that the larynx may be indirectly involved in such non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, including the insertion of an oral‑pharyngeal airway, is important for clinicians when assessing airway management and potential procedural complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1120
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on the larynx and trachea are classified as a subset of the broader category “other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum.” Within this same overarching group, tracheoscopy performed via a tracheostomy is regarded as equivalent to those other diagnostic procedures, highlighting its role as a key endoscopic assessment of the airway. Therapeutic injections directed at the larynx, as well as injections of locally‑acting substances into the trachea, are identified as forms of throat surgery. In the taxonomy used, throat surgery is also placed under the umbrella of “other diagnostic procedures of the respiratory tract and mediastinum,” reflecting the clinical overlap where certain surgical interventions serve both diagnostic and therapeutic purposes in the upper airway. This integrated classification underscores the interconnected nature of diagnostic endoscopy and therapeutic injection techniques in managing laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2325
retrieved_docs.content: The trachea serves as the anatomical site for tracheostomy procedures, and these procedures may be performed as either temporary or permanent interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 789
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic procedures on larynx and trachea are situated within the broader category of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, indicating that examinations of the larynx and trachea are considered part of the general set of respiratory‑tract diagnostic activities. Therapeutic interventions such as Injection of larynx and Injection of locally‑acting therapeutic substance into trachea are each classified as throat surgery, reflecting that these injections are surgical procedures performed on the upper airway. Although throat surgery is primarily a therapeutic classification, it is linked to the larger diagnostic group of other diagnostic procedures of respiratory tract and mediastinum, highlighting that certain interventions on the larynx and trachea can serve both diagnostic and therapeutic roles. Recognizing this overlap helps clinicians select appropriate procedures and coordinate care for patients requiring evaluation or treatment of laryngeal and tracheal conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of larynx and injection of locally-acting therapeutic substance into trachea are both classified as throat surgery, representing surgical interventions that involve delivering agents directly into the laryngeal or tracheal structures of the airway.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2333
retrieved_docs.content: Injection of the larynx includes the subsequent step of تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway), indicating that airway management is an integral component of the laryngeal injection procedure. This relationship underscores that when clinicians perform a laryngeal injection, they also consider establishing an oral‑pharyngeal airway to maintain airway patency and support effective ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1907
retrieved_docs.content: The larynx serves as an indirect procedure site for other non‑or therapeutic procedures on the respiratory system. The specific intervention “Injection of larynx” includes as a subordinate step the procedure “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” (placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway). Likewise, the broader category “other non‑or therapeutic procedures on respiratory system” also lists “تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي” as a child procedure. This demonstrates that placement of an oral‑pharyngeal airway is a common subprocedure both within targeted laryngeal injections and within general non‑therapeutic respiratory interventions, underscoring its relevance to airway management across multiple procedural contexts.

retrieved_docs.content: also for axillary masses (نقائل إبطية). Together, these relationships illustrate how the anatomical location of the trachea within the thoracic cavity interconnects diagnostic categories, surgical classifications, and therapeutic interventions such as intubation, tracheostomy, and airway insertion, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding and managing airway and respiratory tract conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1735
retrieved_docs.content: Trachea and bronchus operations represent a broad category of surgical and procedural interventions involving the airway, and within this category the specific procedure of تنبيب الرغامى (tracheal intubation) is included.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2160
retrieved_docs.content: Tracheoscopy via tracheostomy is performed directly on the trachea, making the trachea the direct procedural site for this endoscopic examination. In contrast, tracheostomy—whether performed as a temporary or permanent airway access—affects the trachea indirectly, serving as an indirect procedural site. Understanding the distinction between direct and indirect procedural involvement of the trachea helps clinicians select appropriate techniques for airway management and diagnostic evaluation.
